parts.title: Transcript
parts.annotated_transcript_html:
Speaker 1: You now have some time to look at the questions on your screen. Now answer the questions on your screen.

Speaker 2: Deciding on the company that was to build the Channel Tunnel was a difficult choice. When the detailed submissions were received, it was agreed that Eurotunnel would be awarded the contract.

Speaker 1: The price tendered was viewed as being very reasonable, but unfortunately the construction cost of the project came in at £4.6 billion, which was actually 80% over the initial budget.

Speaker 2: It was decided that there would be three tunnels under the sea to be used, two for transport and one for maintenance.

Speaker 3: In these tunnels, there are three services available within the Channel Tunnel, which are the Eurostar passenger train, the Euroshuttle, which is a roll-on, roll-off service for road vehicles, and trains designed to carry freight.

Speaker 1: There were also many issues with the British and French workers, and the methods and equipment which they used.

Speaker 2: As work began on each side of the Channel, the idea was to meet in the middle and then expand into the two adjacent tunnels and complete the project.

Speaker 3: However, the French construction teams and the UK construction teams used different methods to seal the tunnel and protect it from the immense pressure created by the sea above. Even though this worked well in the end, there were major concerns that the two methods being used would be incompatible.

Speaker 1: While the teams on both sides of the tunnel started and finished at the same time, in total, the UK construction teams bored out 84 kilometres of tunnel in total, against just 69 kilometres by the French teams.

Speaker 2: The UK and French used the same boring machines, though the UK side used 6 of the mass of tunnel machines, whereas the French side decided to use 5.

Speaker 3: On average, the British workers managed to tunnel around 150 metres a week, whereas the French figure was lower at 110 metres a week, although many put this down to the variety of challenging tunneling conditions.
